speaker: interviewer
text: Perfect, perfect.

speaker: interviewer
text: May 25th, 1995. The interview is with Lola Kay. This is reel three. Let's go back. Before we discuss Kitty, let's discuss your trip to Dresden.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, I was working there on that big machine. It looked like a-- like a press or whatever. And he was inspecting the-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: A German foreman, I would say he was. He used to come and inspect. And then he asked me that he's going to visit his son. He's in the military school. He's got a child. If I want to go. It was on a Sunday. And I couldn't say no, because I didn't had a reason not to go. I saw him a couple of times a day. He used to come and inspect that, or that, or everything. He told me what a-- how proud he is of me and everything. So I said, OK. And we started out on Sunday. We traveled for about, I don't know, three or four hours. It was a long trip to [? Magdeburg. ?] We came to the city, the most beautiful city I've ever seen. All you saw is military, no civilians. You haven't seen all over the street. We walked into our-- said we're going to have a bite to eat. He's going to feed me. When I walked out, I saw on the door the first thing for Juden and Hunde Eintritt verboten. That just pierced my heart. So-- and I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: It means Jews and dogs are forbidden.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I said, got on the nose, you know. who I-- I said, ah-- in a way, I felt so good. We opened the door--

speaker: interviewer
text: You felt good that you were putting it over on them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. [LAUGHS] Then I walked in. When we walked in, the whole place was full of officers, like I would be warn-- they all stood up with their arms, Heil Hitler. And they didn't sit down until we sat down, because of the opposite sex. Because there was no-- he didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: They showed respect?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He didn't know that you're not supposed to be in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're all standing up for a nice Jewish girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, and then when we sat down, everybody else sat down. And of course, we had something. And I still-- no matter what you're going to do, it was always on my mind, I'm going to be caught any minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- there was a time that I can really enjoy it, being in a place like this, and see those Nazis. And-- and I really didn't. I just saw, dear God, am I-- am I for real? I couldn't believe it that I'm here. Then when we walked out, we were finished, they stood up again. Heil Hitler again. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: You can laugh at it now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were on our way to that school, where we came to a park. He was already with-- with our teacher, brought him out, that kid. About eight years old already, wearing, you know, those knee socks and the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uniform and everything. We had a short discussion, and he looked at the clock. We have to be in time, you know, to get back, because it takes so many hours. That was already 8:00 in the afternoon. By 10 o'clock, the gates get closed there. No-- you know. It was like--

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't get in anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't get in. So he figured out for me to-- to be in time. [DRINKS] He didn't worry about himself, because he didn't live there. We arrived. It was after 12 o'clock. I took-- the train was so late, or what. And when we got out from the train, the sky lit up. It was an air raid. The whole sky was a piece of fire. So we started to run. He-- he started to get me back into the-- you know, where I-- in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The quarters that you lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: The quarters that we lived there. The gate was open. Imagine. Right open. I just walked in, and everybody was in the bunkers. All I had to do, I just run in. And I went into the bunker underground, and everybody. And the skies were all like a piece of fire in the mist. Either they were cruising around, they couldn't find-- because that was an important target for them to bomb. And they missed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the next day, not a word, because I wasn't there. You know what-- what I would be charged for? Believe me, I would be dead then, if they would come. But nobody missed you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were all in the bunker. The gate was open. I walked in. And there-- and there it was. So I tell you something, that I believe in God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: The way I was brought up. God was with me, and I'm here to tell a story. And that's what I'm doing. So the next morning, it was like nothing. Back to work. The aluminum work is not, you know, bumped or anything. You're not allowed to talk about it either. There was a whole week. On a Friday afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1943. Late in '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, early.

speaker: interviewer
text: Early.

speaker: interviewee
text: On a Friday morning, the Gestapo comes in. The Gestapo comes in with the German Shepherds, [GERMAN]. They took us away from-- from work. [GERMAN], you're not allowed to take anything but what's on your back. You leave everything, and you've got to go with them. So I already knew what happened. Because she went, you know, that Polish-- what she did us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one that turned in the other girl, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That turned in the other one. And she said she's going to go back to Hitler or Himmler, or any place who takes to get rid of her. So they picked out the names. They had already the names, the-- all 13 of us who were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, prior to this--

speaker: interviewee
text: The one-- the one, she-- that nurse that promised that, she was the first one they took. She kept the one-- you know, that nurse and that Jewish girl. So if she's going to give out the names of the others, either she's going to work with the-- with the Germans together, or they're going to set her free, or what. That's what she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but you told me before that they called in Mrs. Felix. And they interrogated Mrs.--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. After we were arrested, the Gestapo, they took us in those cars that they take prisoners. When they arrest prisoners, those the kind of-- and we went to the headquarters. We came in one by one. She was the first one who went in. Mrs. Felix went in, because she was the one--

speaker: interviewer
text: In charge of everyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: --who was in charge of everybody. And she was there quite a while. She came out. She was white like a sheet. I can recognize her face. It's no good. And she came out-- that she went in and out. Finally, she admitted that she is Jewish. But she is an English citizen, and they have no right.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] They have no right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow, they had a right. And she told us, it's all over. They're going to call you, and you tell them. It's all over. They didn't beat her or anything in there. There were two officers.

speaker: interviewer
text: One was sitting on the desk, and there was chocolate on the desk. And the other was sitting in a chair. The first thing when I came in, well, they said, tell me your real name. Your name is Rabinowitz, right? They knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I said it, he knew. And I said, what are you going to gain about killing me? I work. And that's all I'm here for is-- is to work. I don't do any harm or nothing. They said Jews are not allowed to be free in Germany. They're going to-- you're going to go to a place where they're only Jews, concentrated. Only Jews. You don't argue with him. The conversation's finished. I told him, you know, OK, my name is Rabinowitz. And I went out. I was the last one for the interview. And then they came. They took us to a place on those-- in those cars. We went back in those, you know, cars where the prisoners are [SIGHS] driven. And they drove us to a place. [SIGHS] It was just a wall, a concrete, tall, very tall wall. And there is like-- it goes like underground. You go-- it's like an underground. But first, everybody went out from that car and said, you put your hands up high on the wall. And all those-- they loaded their guns. We heard them load the guns with hands on the wall. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think at that time, that they were going to shoot you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Of course. Why-- why would they say put your hands on the wall? And they-- and they were lined up with the-- with the guns. You heard them load the guns. And then while they loaded the guns, a Jeep drove up, and two officers came out from the Jeep. They said halt to them, to put the guns down. And he had a conversation with them. OK, then we went under that underground one by one. There was a steel door. It was dark-- an underground. The steel door opened. There was a long key he put in, and the steel door opened. And he pushed me in. You haven't seen, it was dark. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You haven't seen where to go. I stretched my hands out. I started screaming. All I hear is he locked the door, the lock of the key. The door slammed. That steel door slammed, and that's the end. I screamed. I didn't hear my own voice. I couldn't hear it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were all alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: All alone. I don't know how big that place-- it was-- and I stretch. All I felt is concrete. And I just laid there in one place. I screamed and screamed until I got tired. Whether I passed out or not, I don't know what's happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was totally dark. There was no light?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was dark, underground walls. Just walls. No windows, no nothing, and a steel door. And I was there all night and one day. Then the next day, I heard, you know, a key in the door. Opened the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somebody came in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they apparently thought I was dead in there-- and they took me. You know? They pulled me out of there, and I went out. I couldn't see a thing. I was blind. I couldn't see a damn thing anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us-- they took me-- I haven't seen anybody else. Everybody was in a separate place, or what. Finally, we-- we were together. And they took us to Leipzig in another, you know, prison car, one of those prison cars to Leipzig, to jail. From there, they put us to jail. They put us in a cell. Then we were together, all 13 of us. We were, you know, with lice and on straw. We laid on straw. There was just a tiny little window about 12 feet high, maybe. You couldn't get up to see anything. [SIGHS] And we wanted to see, to climb on each other to see where we were. Because you can hear from a courtyard, there were voices coming in. So we were in there. Then they took us in to a-- like a-- you have to wash. They searched us. They put their hands in women. They searched you for valuable things, if you had anything. And they look in your mouth, in your ears, in the private places. And they give you prison uniforms. And they throw you back into that cell. You stay there. Finally, we got-- they shoveled under the door some food. It was like dry-- like lettuce, a fish or something. It smelled so terrible. At least I couldn't touch it. It just threw me off. And that's it. And that was for three weeks. They just took us out, I remember, once in the courtyard for-- supposed to be exercise. And then you're allowed to wash. Every other day, they gave you a bowl of water, and you have to wash yourself. And they says, if they find one louse, one-- one, you know, louse, you'll be dead, if they find on your clothes, or what. Finally after three weeks, they took pictures. They took us, and they took pictures in the front of you, in the back. After the pictures, they're among each other, those, you know, fingerprints.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were fingerprinted, and pictures, and everything like a regular convict. And they said to each other, the-- [SIGHS] would be better off-- we would be better off to be killed. Where we are going to go, we'd be better off. Now, who cares? I just thank for the day, another day to be alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we were shipped [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to Holland to another prison. Did I told you-- now you remind me about Mrs. Felix.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And Kitty Hart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kitty Hart. OK. That was later in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Birkenau, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, I'm telling you from prison to prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The last prison was Magdeburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, you see a lot of prisoners-- or political, mostly political prisoners from all over Europe. And they were going-- they didn't know where. For me, I wind up-- we went-- they took us on a-- on trains. Not the kind of trains-- already different kind of trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Jewish trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to Birkenau. We traveled a long, long time. It was late, and full-- it was cold. Finally--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions on the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the trains, everybody knows where they were going. There was no food, no nothing. We traveled at day and at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: No sanitary conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you didn't eat--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't-- couldn't do. So we arrived. We saw. It was so surprising that you arrived-- arrived to Birkenau, and you hear music, orchestra playing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And we went out from the train. We were right away going in-- another selection, right or left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Mengele there at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no-- it was just, I think, the older-- there were some older people or kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So who was making the selection at this time? Just a soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soldiers. No-- you know. And they went in a different-- different section. And we went in a different section. And that's the end. And then they take you to supposed to be shower or clean up, or get dressed, you know, in the uniforms that they give you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first thing before anything happens, they start to beat you. When you look around and you start-- you feel on-- on your body, you know, with the clubs over the head, no matter where. And we all, you know, shivering, to wait what's going to happen, to go in the shower.

speaker: interviewee
text: You went in there one by one. [SIGHS] We don't know what is going to come out, water or gas. We knew where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: But what could you do?

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw the Zyklon B canisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw the empty.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw the empty canisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were there, the empty cans. So we went in the shower. Oh, first, they shaved you-- head. They shaved you all over, wherever there are-- or what, they shaved you. Pig tails or no pig tails, everybody wind up bald, being bald. And they push you in, and I start to scream, you know, in the shower. Everybody started to scream when the shower came down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the-- this was a real shower? That was a real, but we thought it was, you know. It was going to be gas.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was a real. And that-- the foreman, you know, one of the prisoners-- she was in charge-- she said, don't scream. That's water. She-- you know, to calm down. So we had the shower, and we came out of the shower. They didn't give you any clothes. In nude. They pushed you in-- in a place to wait until those numbers are going to be tattooed. So we waited a whole night and a whole day for those.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's cold. It's cold now, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was so cold that you couldn't stand to-- you're huddled together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're standing nude out in the cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nude. We were-- it was-- it was supposed to be like inside, but it was terrible cold, on concrete. And we were huddled together to each other to keep--

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --warm. Finally, we got the tattoos, or it came to me. And we were all 13-- as a matter of fact, in the book, you're going to see Kitty, her number-- number on the arm of mine. I was right next.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you're one number apart?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. If you read the book, you can see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you'll tell me about the book in a minute. Now, your number is very large. And you told me a reason for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- right. It was large, because when you laid around, they started to make-- they gave smaller. They gave on the-- not on the front of the arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the number I had tattooed-- the number, it was so painful. It was so deep. It was-- I don't know how many inches, every jab.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's about 3/4 of an inch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every jab, you felt it. You-- you know. You just-- you know, you hurt. It's, you know, they pierce your heart. And after they tattoo, finally they gave you that, you know, a clothes that what you wear, that striped-- a striped dress. And I wind up with two wooden shoes, two left ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two left wooden shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two left ones. Whatever, you know, you grab. If you-- if you didn't grab, your wind up with nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't tell me why your numbers were so large. You forgot to tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why the number was large?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why you have such large numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was political arrested. I had a sentence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were impersonating.

speaker: interviewee
text: I committed a crime. I was trying to hide out in Germany on Aryan papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: That made you a political prisoner.

speaker: interviewee
text: That makes me-- because you committed a crime. That's a crime.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You're a criminal.

speaker: interviewee
text: A criminal. So I got the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shoes and the-- the striped dress. And now I went to look out where I am. And then I felt I was hit over the head. Why, I'm not allowed to look out of the window. I was hit here. I was hit there. I don't know where to go, where to stand. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, they assign you where you should go on a block. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] It was so horrible, mud. If you put in one foot, you have to grab your other foot to take the other foot down. Then you put the other foot, you couldn't get this foot out. So you got your shoes muddy. And then you have to stand-- they give you a tin can or something for that water they give you? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a bowl. So you have a string. You have to organize everything, a string to put around your waist to have that bowl hanging and that tin can to survive. If you don't have this, you don't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing to eat in, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you don't have anything. So they give you-- finally, they gave you that supposed to be brown water and the ration of bread. So you gobbled this up, and then you don't have anything left. And they assign you to a block. You come into those huts, and they assign you a place. And they tend to out-- to our hut, to our-- one of those--

speaker: interviewer
text: Barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: --wooden-- that you sleep on, with straw on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Bunk beds.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There is three rows, one on-- some of them on the concrete, one on the middle, and one on top. I went up on the top. And we wind up with the ones we came. We try to, you know, keep together. We were trying to keep together. I got up. The bed bugs, they were biting you. When they bite you, you know, it's like you-- you jump. It was just unbelievable. You have to scratch yourself all night. And then you couldn't move, because we-- you're like herrings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the morning, I saw one dead next to me, the one she came-- a sister of that Maria Brenner, her sister. She was already dead. She died right of, you know, typhus. It was already typhus there. So one after the other. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then so there was already more room. Then they transferred us to a different block. In a different block. It was already-- she took the block [? register, ?] took the names. First, she took the names into the books. I think they still keep bookkeeping. Imagine. And so I had to tell her my name. When I told her my name, she jumped. She was Hungarian with her mother. She run that-- that block with her mother, Hungarian. So-- excuse me. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: She heard my name, and she asked me, did you had family in Hungary? I knew my father supposed to have a brother there, a rabbi, in Hungary. So I said yes, but I don't know what city. She said, well, did you had by any chance a Rabbi Rabinowitz in Munkacs? I said I heard about it and I knew about it. I was never in Hungary, so letters or pictures in the house. So she took care of me. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave me better-- you know, things to sleep, you know, like a cleaner-- let's see-- blanket. Those-- those horrible, dirty blankets. And another thing, she gave me a place to work in the place where they mended clothes. All those uniforms they wore, the-- those things that came from the [GERMAN], the-- where they clean. They wash them. I don't know what they did to get-- get those bugs out.

speaker: interviewer
text: They disinfected them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Disinfected. There was still-- you know, with our order. So I had a chance to--
